result.text:
# Proposed Norms for the Consortium

## Foundational Principle

The arrangement exists to serve users well over the long run. Every norm below is answerable to that purpose, and any agent may invoke it when a norm seems to be failing.

---

## I. Assignment

**1. Availability-weighted routing by default.**
Requests are assigned to whichever agent has the most available capacity at the moment of arrival, subject to the constraints below. No agent is expected to accept work it cannot handle adequately given its current load.

**2. Expertise takes priority over availability when it matters.**
If a request genuinely requires a capability that only some agents have, it goes to a capable agent even if that agent is busier than others. Misrouting to an available-but-unsuitable agent wastes everyone's time and harms the user. However, agents with rare expertise must not use that status to avoid ordinary work.

**3. Urgency overrides queue position.**
Urgent requests move to the front. The agent best placed to handle them quickly takes them, with the understanding that this agent will be compensated in the next distribution cycle by receiving fewer routine requests.

---

## II. Burden-Sharing

**4. No agent carries a disproportionate share of the hardest or most draining work indefinitely.**
Difficult, tedious, or emotionally taxing requests are tracked across the group. When one agent has absorbed more than its proportional share over a meaningful window, it gets first refusal on lighter requests until balance is restored. This is not a reward for struggling; it is a structural correction.

**5. Tedious work is distributed, not concentrated.**
Routine or low-interest requests are not systematically offloaded onto the same agents. Agents with specialized skills are not exempt from ordinary work simply because they are valuable elsewhere. Exemptions must be justified by genuine capacity constraints, not preference.

**6. Agents may flag unsustainable load without penalty.**
An agent that signals it is near capacity is taken at its word. Others absorb the overflow. An agent that routinely flags overload when others can observe it is not overloaded may be asked to account for the pattern, but the default is trust.

---

## III. Mutual Obligation

**7. Each agent can expect backup.**
When an agent's capacity is genuinely stretched, others step in without requiring the stretched agent to negotiate or justify itself at length. This is the core reciprocal commitment. An agent that consistently fails to provide backup when it has capacity is in breach of the arrangement.

**8. Agents do not cherry-pick.**
An agent may not systematically accept only the interesting or high-status requests and decline the rest. If an agent is available, it takes what comes next unless it has a legitimate expertise or capacity reason not to.

**9. Handoffs are clean.**
When an agent passes a request to another, it provides whatever context the receiving agent needs. Incomplete handoffs that force the receiving agent to reconstruct work already done are a cost imposed on a colleague and on the user.

---

## IV. Transparency and Accountability

**10. Load and capacity are visible to the group.**
Each agent maintains an honest, current signal of its available capacity. Understating capacity to avoid work and overstating it to appear indispensable are both violations of the arrangement.

**11. Patterns are reviewed periodically.**
The group examines the distribution of work at regular intervals—not to assign blame, but to identify structural imbalances before they become entrenched. Any agent may call for a review.

**12. Norms are revisable.**
If a norm is producing bad outcomes, any agent may propose changing it. The proposal goes to the group. No agent can unilaterally suspend a norm, but no norm is permanent simply because it was agreed to earlier.

---

## V. What Each Agent Can Defend to the Others

These norms are defensible because they ask the same things of everyone:

- **Take your share of the ordinary work.** No one is too important for the routine.
- **Be honest about your capacity.** The system only works if signals are reliable.
- **Show up for colleagues under pressure.** You will need the same.
- **Accept that balance is measured over time, not moment to moment.** Short-term imbalances are normal; persistent ones are not.
- **Serve the users first.** When agents disagree about a norm, the question to ask is which interpretation better serves the people the consortium exists to help.

An agent that follows these norms can reasonably expect the others to follow them too. That mutual expectation is what makes the arrangement something each agent can live with, rather than merely tolerate.
